title: Raw vs Structured Observations
concept: Parsing raw terminal streams or HTML into structured data that language models can reason over effectively.

exercises.prompt: Quick start! An AI agent is a program that can look at its surroundings and take actions on its own. What is the FIRST thing an agent needs to do before acting?

options.text: Observe what's going on around it

exercises.explain: Before an agent can decide what to do, it has to observe its environment — read the screen, the page, or the data. This lesson is all about how it does that well.

cards.title: A Wall of Text
cards.body: Imagine an agent opening a webpage and getting 5,000 messy lines of code all at once. That's a raw observation — hard to make sense of. Good agents clean it up first.

cards.title: Raw vs Structured
cards.body: A RAW observation is messy, unfiltered data like terminal output or HTML code. A STRUCTURED observation is that same info tidied into neat, labeled pieces an AI can easily read.

cards.title: Laundry Basket vs Drawers
cards.body: Raw data is like a basket of tangled clothes dumped together. Structured data is like a dresser where socks, shirts, and pants each have their own drawer. Much easier to find things!

cards.title: Reading a Shopping Page
cards.body: Raw: a jumble of HTML tags around a price. Structured: {"item": "Milk", "price": "$3"}. The agent instantly knows the name and the price with no guessing.

cards.title: "More Data Is Always Better"
cards.body: Not true! Dumping tons of raw text can confuse a model and waste its limited attention. Clean, structured data helps it reason far better than a giant messy pile.

cards.title: Two Ways to See the World
cards.body: Same information, two very different forms. One overwhelms the model; one helps it focus.

exercises.prompt: Why do agents often turn raw HTML or terminal output into structured data before the language model reads it?

options.text: So the model can find the important parts easily and reason better
To make the webpage load faster
Because models cannot read any English at all
To permanently delete the original page

exercises.explain: Structured data labels and organizes the key facts, so the model spends its attention on what matters instead of digging through messy code.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A raw terminal stream is usually already in the tidiest, most organized form for an AI to reason over.
exercises.explain: False! Raw streams are messy and unfiltered. Parsing them into structured pieces first makes them much easier for a model to reason over.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about tidying observations.
exercises.sentence: Turning messy HTML into neat, labeled pieces is called making the data structured.
exercises.explain: Structured data is organized into clear, labeled pieces — the opposite of raw, messy output.

exercises.prompt: Which observation was cleaned up (parsed into structured data) by an AI agent?

options.text: bash: err 0x2f loading /tmp/aa9 ... retry? y/n stderr#

exercises.explain: The first option is clean and structured, with clear labels like product, price, and in_stock. The others are raw, unfiltered output straight from HTML or a terminal.

exercises.prompt: An agent must buy the cheapest laptop on a store page. The page returns 3,000 lines of raw HTML. What is the BEST first move so the language model can reason well?

options.text: Parse the HTML into a structured list of laptop names and prices, then let the model compare
Paste all 3,000 raw lines into the model and hope it finds the price
Ignore the page and guess a random laptop
Ask the model to memorize every HTML tag first

exercises.explain: The winning move is to parse the raw HTML into structured data — a clean list of names and prices. This lets the model focus its limited attention on comparing prices instead of getting lost in messy code. Structured observations = clearer reasoning and better actions.
